items.title: 3 CTA bus passengers injured in West Town hit-and-run, Chicago police say
items.summary: Three people on a CTA bus were injured in a hit-and-run crash in West Town Friday morning, Chicago police said.

items.title: Chicago To Honor Folk Legend John Prine With 5-Day Festival
items.summary: "Souvenirs" will run Oct. 7-11 and include concerts, film screenings, tours and more at various venues across the city.

items.title: Gov. Pritzker signs bills to support law enforcement
items.summary: Governor JB Pritzker signed two bills aimed at addressing staffing shortages in law enforcement. One bill lowers age restrictions for sheriff's department hires, allowing individuals as young as 20 to serve as county police officers. The second bill expands penalties for violations of Illinois' Move Over Law, making it more difficult for drivers convicted of causing injury or death to regain their driving privileges.

items.title: Beard Awards' future uncertain
items.summary: The James Beard Foundation is considering changes to its awards program, which has been a significant event for Chicago's culinary scene. Local chefs and restaurants are closely watching the developments, as the awards have historically highlighted the city's vibrant food culture.

items.title: Governor signs bills to support law enforcement
items.summary: Governor JB Pritzker is set to sign two bills aimed at addressing staffing shortages in law enforcement. One bill will lower the age for certain sheriff's department hires to 20, while another will increase penalties for violations of Illinois' Move Over Law, making it tougher for drivers to regain their licenses after causing injury or death.

items.title: Chicago's restaurant scene adapts to challenges
items.summary: Local restaurants are innovating to cope with rising costs and changing consumer preferences. Many are focusing on sustainability and community engagement, with some businesses offering new menu items and experiences to attract customers. This shift is crucial for maintaining the vibrancy of Chicago's dining landscape.

items.title: 27 Things To Do In Chicago This Weekend: West Fest, Tacos Y Tamales And Chicago Climate Week
items.summary: Bevapalooza, Colombian Fest, Taste of River North and sidewalk sales from Avondale to Hyde Park are also on deck.

items.title: Magnolia Bakery Temporarily Closes Downtown After Inspectors Find Live Roaches, Fly Infestation
items.summary: The popular New York-based chain's State Street location has failed three health inspections in the last year, including on Monday.

items.title: Chicago Daily News Building Is Now Officially A Landmark
items.summary: The landmark status will allow the building's owner to apply for city incentives for rehabilitation projects.

items.title: Council OKs $424 Million In Public Funds For Parking And More Near New Fire Stadium
items.summary: The plan to use $250 million of that money on the garage and other improvements to benefit The 78 mega-development sparked lengthy debate this week but was ultimately OK'd.

items.title: James Beard Awards Launch National Search For New Host City
items.summary: Chicago has hosted the culinary awards since 2015 and will continue to do so through 2028. The process to find a new location opened Wednesday.

items.title: Illinois lawmakers face data center scrutiny
items.summary: As data centers expand, Illinois Governor JB Pritzker has halted new tax incentive agreements to address concerns about their impact on local communities. This move aims to balance economic growth with the responsibility to protect residents from rising utility costs and resource strain. Pritzker's administration is pushing for a regulatory framework to ensure that the growth of this industry does not come at the expense of local communities.

items.title: Pritzker signs bill for exoneree compensation
items.summary: Governor JB Pritzker has signed a new law that will provide exonerees with up to $50,000 per year for wrongful imprisonment. This includes compensation for time spent in pretrial detention or juvenile facilities. The legislation aims to support those who have been wrongfully convicted and help them reintegrate into society.

items.title: Magnolia Bakery at Chicago's Block 37 shut down due to cockroaches
items.summary: The Magnolia Bakery location in downtown Chicago was shut down this week due to a cockroach infestation.

items.title: Pritzker signs bill improving compensation for exonerees
items.summary: Governor JB Pritzker has signed a new law that will provide exonerees with up to $50,000 per year for wrongful imprisonment. This compensation includes time spent in pretrial detention or juvenile detention facilities before exoneration, aiming to support those who have suffered due to wrongful convictions.

items.title: Chicago mayoral candidate raises over $1 million
items.summary: Mayoral candidate Joe Holberg has announced that his campaign has raised $330,000 in the second quarter of 2026, bringing his total cash on hand to $1.03 million. This significant fundraising effort highlights the competitive nature of the upcoming mayoral race in Chicago.

items.title: Garden exhibit this weekend
items.summary: A garden exhibit will take place on Saturday and Sunday, July 18th and 19th, from 11 AM to 4 PM at 1059 N. Winchester. Visitors can enjoy new paintings and the garden itself, with parking available on Thomas Street or on the garage apron.

items.title: Chicago's Magnolia Bakery shut down over cockroach infestation
items.summary: The Magnolia Bakery location in downtown Chicago has been closed over a cockroach infestation.

items.title: Printers Row Sports Bar Faces Eviction Amid Noise Complaints, Wage Theft Allegations
items.summary: Since February, neighbors have decried noise and rowdiness at Reserv. The business owner is due at a Thursday Business Affairs and Consumer Protection hearing over a list of changes demanded by the city.

items.title: Sprawling Woodlawn Central Development, Daily News Building Landmarking Get Zoning Approval
items.summary: The City Council's zoning committee also advanced a community center plan in Altgeld Gardens, a large mixed-use project in North Lawndale and various North Side apartment buildings.

items.title: Illinois lawmakers face challenges during summer session
items.summary: Illinois lawmakers are navigating significant issues this summer, including a federal investigation involving state Rep. Carol Ammons. This situation has led to increased scrutiny and discussions about legislative ethics, which could impact local governance and community trust.

items.title: Chicago's assault weapons ban update
items.summary: The Chicago City Council is set to discuss updates to the assault weapons ban, which could affect local gun regulations. This discussion comes amid ongoing debates about public safety and community protection in the city.

items.title: Local businesses brace for trade tariff impacts
items.summary: Chicago businesses are preparing for potential challenges due to ongoing trade conflicts, particularly with European partners. The uncertainty surrounding tariffs could affect local industries that rely on international trade, highlighting the need for stable economic policies.

items.title: 34th Ward community cleanup scheduled
items.summary: A community cleanup event is planned for July 20, 2026, in the 34th Ward. Residents are encouraged to participate in beautifying local parks and streets, fostering community spirit and environmental responsibility.

items.title: 34th Ward Neighborhood Association meeting
items.summary: The 34th Ward Neighborhood Association will hold its monthly meeting on July 25, 2026, at the Greektown Community Center. Residents are invited to discuss local issues, upcoming events, and community initiatives.

items.title: New restaurant opening in Greektown
items.summary: A new Mediterranean restaurant is set to open in Greektown on July 30, 2026. The establishment aims to bring fresh dining options to the area, contributing to the local economy and culinary scene.

items.title: Summer festival in Printers Row
items.summary: The Printers Row Summer Festival will take place on August 5-6, 2026, featuring local artists, food vendors, and live music. This event aims to celebrate community culture and support local businesses.

items.title: Trade conflict poses challenges for Illinois businesses
items.summary: A trade conflict between the U.S. and the EU could impact Illinois businesses that rely on European trade. European companies have invested over $100 billion in Illinois, and the uncertainty introduced by tariffs is a major concern for companies planning future investments.

items.title: Assault weapons ban discussions continue
items.summary: The ongoing discussions about assault weapons bans in Illinois are significant for community safety. Local leaders are considering various measures to address gun violence, which remains a pressing issue for residents.

items.title: Garden exhibit and new paintings this weekend
items.summary: A garden exhibit featuring new paintings will take place on July 18th and 19th at 1059 N. Winchester from 11 AM to 4 PM. Visitors can park on Thomas St. or on the garage apron and enter through the garden gate.

items.title: Plan For Loop’s 2nd Weed Dispensary Could Go Up In Smoke
items.summary: Contra LLC's plans to open in the Loop is facing obstacles, including a missed community meeting requirement and a court ruling over the state’s 1,500-foot distancing rule.

items.title: Octet,' Raven Theatre’s Musical About 8 Internet-Addicted Adults, Is Headed To The Goodman
items.summary: The Edgewater theater's popular a cappella musical opens Wednesday on the Goodman's Owen Stage Downtown.

items.title: 42 Parks On South, West Sides Getting Improved Air Conditioning To Help Battle Heat Waves
items.summary: The park fieldhouses set to get additional or improved air conditioning systems were chosen using the Chicago Heat Vulnerability Index to identify areas that suffer the most during heat waves.

items.title: Mayor moves to reclaim control of the CHA board
items.summary: Mayor Brandon Johnson is moving to reassert control over the Chicago Housing Authority board after a stretch of independence, per Crain's. The CHA oversees public housing across the city, and the shake-up lands as former CHA board chair Matt Brewer runs against Johnson for mayor.

items.title: Stockyards' new era
items.summary: The historic Union Stockyards site is undergoing a transformation into a mixed-use development, featuring residential units, retail spaces, and parks. This redevelopment aims to honor the site's rich history while providing modern amenities for residents and visitors. The project is expected to create numerous jobs and stimulate local businesses in the area.

items.title: IGWL's Weekly Action Plan
items.summary: Indivisible Greater West Loop is organizing a community meeting on July 20, 2026, to discuss local issues and strategies for civic engagement. Residents are encouraged to attend and share their concerns about neighborhood development and public safety. The meeting will take place at the West Loop Community Center, located at 1234 W. Madison St.

items.title: Suspect causes damage at Loop nightclub during break-in, Chicago police say
items.summary: Chicago police are investigating a break-in at a nightclub in the Loop. The front door glass of the business was broken and so were the windows, and the suspect also caused damage inside.

items.title: Burglars break into store in Chicago's West Town community, but fail to get ATM
items.summary: Burglars tried unsuccessfully to take an ATM from a store in Chicago's West Town community early Monday, police said.

items.title: Police investigate burglary in Chicago's West Town community
items.summary: Burglars tried unsuccessfully to take an ATM from a store in Chicago’s West Town community early Monday.

items.title: Near West Side Chicago church commemorates speech by Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.
items.summary: A congregation on Chicago's Near West Side gathered over the weekend to commemorate 60 years since Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. spoke there.

items.title: At Downtown Day,' Chicago Teens Learn That The City Center Belongs To Everybody'
items.summary: Amid larger talks over "teen takeovers" and other gatherings, My Block, My Hood, My City's "Downtown Day" aims to provide kids with a structured and positive way to experience parts of the city they rarely visit.

items.title: 2 women, man shot on Chicago's Near West Side
items.summary: Three people were wounded early Sunday morning in a shooting on Chicago's Near West Side.

items.title: 2 women shot in head, man shot in leg on Chicago's Near West Side
items.summary: Three people were wounded early Sunday morning in a shooting on Chicago's Near West Side.

items.title: Chicago police investigate gunfire involving officers in Grant Park
items.summary: Chicago police early Sunday were investigating gunfire involving one of their own officers in Grant Park in downtown Chicago.

items.title: COPA investigates shooting involving CPD officers in Chicago's Grant Park
items.summary: Chicago police early Sunday were investigating gunfire involving one of their own officers in Grant Park in downtown Chicago. Asal Rezaei reports.

items.title: My Block My Hood My City holds 4th Downtown Day for hundreds of teens
items.summary: Each teenager received a Downtown Day T-shirt, a $50 prepaid debit card to support local businesses and access to dozens of destinations across the loop and other downtown neighborhoods.

items.title: West Loop Art Fest: Mark Your Calendar for August 22 & 23
items.summary: Join us August 22 & 23 as Fulton Street between Carpenter and Green Street transforms into a vibrant outdoor art experience in the heart of Chicago's West Loop. Explore original artwork from talented artists, watch live art demonstrations, discover interactive experiences, and support the creative community that makes our neighborhood so unique.

items.title: New Member Spotlight: Fred Astaire Dance Studio West Loop
items.summary: Located at 943 W Randolph Street, Fred Astaire Dance Studio West Loop offers ballroom and Latin dance instruction for individuals and couples. Their programs include private lessons, group classes, and social dance opportunities, helping students of all experience levels learn something new and connect through dance.

items.title: New Member Spotlight: Stepping Stones Nursery School West Loop
items.summary: Stepping Stones Nursery School West Loop, located at 1025 W Van Buren Street, is a Reggio-inspired early childhood education center serving children from 6 weeks to 5 years old. Their curriculum emphasizes language-rich learning and sensory exploration, providing a nurturing environment for children's growth.

items.title: Aldermen reject Mayor Brandon Johnson’s move to ask voters if millionaires should be taxed
items.summary: The City Council Rules Committee advanced four non-binding referendum proposals for the November ballot, but turned down Mayor Brandon Johnson’s bid to gauge voter support for a 3 percent tax on millionaires. This decision reflects ongoing debates about taxation and funding in Chicago.

items.title: Hunt for Good Raises Over $41,000 to Support Chicago Youth
items.summary: The WCA supported The Simple Good's 11th Annual Hunt for Good Chicago Art Scavenger Hunt, raising $41,120 for arts-based social-emotional learning programs. The event engaged 160 participants and completed 430 acts of kindness across seven neighborhoods.

items.title: Formento's Summer Private Dining Offer
items.summary: Enjoy 15% off all food and beverage for any event booked and consumed in July at Formento's. This offer is valid for corporate gatherings, birthday celebrations, and other special occasions, providing elegant private dining spaces and customizable menus.

items.title: Get Your Cycling Relay Team Ready!
items.summary: The Charity Divvy Relay during the xXx Racing Fulton Market Grand Prix invites teams of one to four riders to cycle through Fulton Market while raising funds for bike safety and community programs. Sign up your team today to participate in this unique event.

items.title: Project Windows 2026 Now on Display Across Chicago
items.summary: Project Windows 2026 transforms storefronts into vibrant public art installations inspired by the Art Institute of Chicago's exhibition. This year's program features over 100 participating window displays, including several in the West Loop, encouraging community engagement and artistic appreciation.

items.title: Jesse White Tumblers Stun Judges On America’s Got Talent,' Even Simon Cowell
items.summary: The youth tumbling team started by former Illinois Secretary of State Jesse White impressed the judges on the reality TV competition Tuesday.

items.title: Upon His Return To The Goodman, Nick Offerman Reflects On His Early Days In Chicago Theater
items.summary: The Chicago-area native stars in “Iceboy!” with his wife, Megan Mullally, and performs a one-night solo show Monday at the Ramova in Bridgeport.

items.title: Chicago Sky DJ Ca$hEra Is Making It Big By Staying True To Herself
items.summary: CaSera Heining, a queer Black female DJ in a male-dominated industry, is in her second full season as the Sky's DJ and spins records at Bulls games and Nike HQ. "She definitely has an energy about her that commands attention."

items.title: MUBI Fest Returns With Screenings At 3 Iconic Chicago Theaters
items.summary: Tickets for the July 10-12 movie festival are on sale now. The movie lineup includes "The Substance," "Sleepaway Camp" and more.

items.title: Fine Arts Building Welcomes Groundswell Coffee In Space Next To Former Artist’s Cafe
items.summary: DOWNTOWN - Chicago-based Groundswell Coffee Roasters is opening inside the Fine Arts Building this month, seven years after the building's original cafe closed. Located at 410 S.

items.title: Want To Watch The World Cup At Recess? Get In Line
items.summary: Fans from all over the world have been flocking to the West Loop bar, with its massive patio and jumbotron screens, despite the lines that stretch around the block. The bar's guest count is up 300 percent.

items.title: Bud Rita's Opens Loop's First Weed Shop As City Eases Restrictions For Downtown Dispensaries
items.summary: LOOP - The Loop’s first legal cannabis dispensary is now open, two years after Downtown Ald. Bill Conway (34th) changed city rules to make it easier for weed shops to fill vacant storefronts in the Central Business District. Bud & Rita’s, located inside a former Bank of America at 105 W.

items.title: Meet The Man Behind The Sky Ride Tap, The Loop's Last Real Dive Bar
items.summary: THE LOOP - If you've ever been to the Sky Ride Tap, you've undoubtedly seen Kyriakos "Carl" Damianides. Since he bought the bar in 1972, he's been a near-constant presence inside. "For the first 25 years, I worked from 7 a.m. to 2 a.m., seven days a week," he said.

items.title: Chicago Vegan Farmers Market Moving To West Loop
items.summary: WEST LOOP - Chicago's only all-vegan farmers market is moving to the West Loop, with weekly markets starting Friday in Skinner Park. The Chicago Vegan Farmers Market launched last year in West Town and is relocating to Skinner Park, 1331 W. Monroe St., organizers announced in a news release.

items.title: Shedd Aquarium Rescuing 10 Beluga Whales From Shuttered Canadian Park
items.summary: The beluga whales will be flown to their new homes as part of an international rescue effort unprecedented in scale, according to Dr. Karisa Tang, vice president of animal health at the Shedd.

items.title: 30 Things To Do In Chicago This Weekend: Taste Of Chicago, Square Roots, Chosen Few DJs And More
items.summary: Windy City Smokeout, MUBI Fest, the Beverly-Morgan Park Garden Walk and multiple markets are also on deck.

items.title: Chief Keef Will Perform In Chicago For 1st Time In 14 Years With Show At Northerly Island
items.summary: After effectively being blacklisted from performing in the city for over a decade, the South Side rapper will return to Chicago on Sep. 12 as part of his nationwide tour for his latest mixtape, "Skeletor."

items.title: 'Live Launch' brings a modern mall to the West Loop
items.summary: A new retail concept called Live Launch has opened at 810 W. Washington Blvd, blending online and in-person shopping. Eleven storefronts of online-based brands share a warehouse-like space where you can touch, try on, and check products, then scan a QR code to buy. Current tenants include the Chicago Sky's first standalone shop, Lux & Nyx handbags, and Surreal Cycling.

items.title: City Council panel rejects millionaire-tax ballot question
items.summary: The City Council Rules Committee advanced seven non-binding referendum questions for the November and February ballots but turned down Mayor Brandon Johnson's bid to ask voters about a 3 percent tax on millionaires, per the Tribune. The advisory questions let voters weigh in without changing law directly.

items.title: Illinois officials watch mail-ballot rules ahead of 2026 vote
items.summary: Illinois Democrats and Attorney General Kwame Raoul are pressing the U.S. Postal Service over proposed changes to mail-in voting, after a federal judge blocked parts of a Trump executive order on elections. Mail ballots made up roughly 30 percent of votes cast in 2024, so any change to processing timelines could affect voters before the 2026 midterms.

items.title: Mayor names new city public health commissioner
items.summary: Mayor Brandon Johnson has tapped Dr. Garth Walker, chief medical officer at Rush Health, to lead the Chicago Department of Public Health. Walker would replace Dr. Olusimbo Ige, who resigned in May. The appointment requires City Council approval.

items.title: Ground broken on Chicago Torture Justice Memorial
items.summary: Survivors, advocates, Mayor Johnson and Ald. Jeanette Taylor broke ground in Washington Park on the future Chicago Torture Justice Memorial, honoring victims of police torture under Commander Jon Burge and his 'Midnight Crew' from 1972 to 1991. The 2015 city reparations package that recognized the torture included a formal apology, a $5.5 million victims fund, and a CPS curriculum.

items.title: Illinois health officials track a stomach-bug outbreak
items.summary: State health officials are investigating an outbreak of cyclosporiasis, a gastrointestinal illness caused by a microscopic parasite spread through contaminated food. As of Tuesday, 141 cases had been reported. Past outbreaks have been linked to raspberries, basil, salad mixes and cilantro, so experts recommend washing produce under running water before eating.

items.title: CTA reports crime down six months in a row
items.summary: Crime on the CTA has dropped for six consecutive months amid a security surge, the agency's leadership says, per the Sun-Times. The trend covers the transit lines and stations that serve the West Loop, Loop and South Loop.

items.title: Dr. Lisa Nee makes her run for mayor official
items.summary: Cardiologist and VA whistleblower Dr. Lisa Nee formally announced her 2027 mayoral campaign. While working at Hines VA Hospital she spoke out about unnecessary procedures, delays in care and mismanagement, and says she faced retaliation for doing so. She joins a crowded field that already includes Alexi Giannoulias, Matthew Brewer, Susana Mendoza and Mike Quigley.

items.title: Chicago pays double the suburbs to remove lead water lines
items.summary: An Axios analysis finds Chicago pays about $31,000 to replace a single lead service line while Evanston spends under $15,000, Elgin $10,950 and a Riverside bid came in at $9,200, all using the same local contractors. Chicagoans will shoulder much of the estimated $12 billion cost of removing a record 400,000 lead lines over the next two decades. City water officials did not respond to requests for an explanation of the gap.

items.title: City budget fight sharpens over $130 million gap claim
items.summary: Mayor Brandon Johnson says the alternative budget provisions pushed by a group of aldermen he calls the 'corporate caucus' come up $130 million short. Ald. Matt O'Shea countered that the administration is sabotaging the alternative plan. The dispute sets the tone for a contentious fall budget season at City Hall.

items.title: Police department blows past budget for fifth straight year
items.summary: The Chicago Police Department overspent its 2025 budget by $162.5 million, the fifth consecutive year of overruns, with more than $120 million of the overage going to personnel costs, per WTTW. The Obama Presidential Center also releases a new batch of tickets today.

items.title: Navy Pier offers free Centennial Wheel rides after foggy Fourth
items.summary: Navy Pier is giving away free Centennial Wheel rides to make up for the July 4th fireworks show that fog largely obscured. Free-ride tickets are available through Navy Pier while the offer lasts.

items.title: Storm cleanup: what Chicago homeowners and drivers need to know
items.summary: Heavy July Fourth weekend storms knocked out power for nearly 300,000 ComEd customers, flooded homes and CTA buses, and downed trees across the city, and Chicago's Deep Tunnel floodwater system came close to full for the first time in its history. Axios rounds up the insurance basics: if a tree hits your house or car, contact your insurer promptly and document the damage with photos, and remember property insurance typically covers removal and repair only when a structure is actually hit.

items.title: Giannoulias urges residents to report license-plate tampering by federal agents
items.summary: Illinois Secretary of State Alexi Giannoulias is asking residents to report immigration agents who remove or obscure vehicle license plates, after a video appearing to show an agent with a plateless SUV went viral. He said operating a vehicle without the required plates is illegal in Illinois for everyone, and pointed residents to a tip line at 312-814-1730 and platewatch@ilsos.gov.

items.title: Illinois becomes the first state to require independent AI safety audits
items.summary: Gov. JB Pritzker signed the Artificial Intelligence Safety Measures Act, which supporters call the nation's strongest state-level AI safety law. It requires the largest AI developers (frontier labs with more than $500 million in revenue) to publish risk frameworks, undergo annual third-party audits, and report serious safety incidents within 72 hours. Attorney General Kwame Raoul's office will enforce it, with penalties up to $1 million for a first violation. The bill passed the House 110-0 and the Senate 52-5.

items.source_name: Indivisible Greater West Loop (Weekly Action Plan)
items.title: 'Good Trouble Lives On' weekend of action, July 17-19
items.summary: Indivisible Greater West Loop is joining a national weekend honoring the late Rep. John Lewis and voting rights, July 17-19. Local events include a candlelight vigil near Buckingham Fountain (Fri, July 17), plus a voter registration table at Mary Bartelme Park and a volunteer shift at State Sen. Lakesia Collins' Summer Block Party in North Lawndale (Sat, July 18).

items.source_name: Indivisible Greater West Loop (Weekly Action Plan)
items.title: IGWL mid-July calendar: book club, food depository, ice cream social and more
items.summary: Upcoming with Indivisible Greater West Loop: Book Club (July 8), volunteering at the Greater Chicago Food Depository (Fri, July 10, 12:45-3:30pm), a family-friendly Popsicle & Ice Cream Social (Sat, July 11, 11am-1pm), the July Chapter Meetup with guest speaker Nick Uniejewski (Mon, July 13, 6-8pm), and Drinks for Democracy postcard writing (Wed, July 15). IGWL also tables at the West Loop Farmers Market on Saturdays, 8-11:30am.

items.title: Taste of Chicago returns to Grant Park, July 8-12
items.summary: Taste of Chicago is back in Grant Park (Columbus Drive from Balbo to Monroe) July 8-12, with dozens of food vendors, nationally recognized musical performances, local artists, family attractions, and culinary demonstrations across the festival grounds.

items.title: 'Youth Voice, Real Change' Summit at UIC, July 25
items.summary: Registration is open through July 17 for the Chicago Youth Summit on Saturday, July 25 at 11am at UIC Student Center East (750 S. Halsted). The afternoon focuses on youth leadership, creativity, and community impact, with space for young people to connect with peers and explore ways to lead change in their communities.

items.title: Police Superintendent Larry Snelling announces retirement
items.summary: CPD Superintendent Larry Snelling will retire July 15 after more than three decades with the department. Mayor Johnson's first pick to lead CPD, Snelling oversaw a historic drop in homicides and closer collaboration with violence-prevention groups. Fred Waller will serve as interim superintendent, reprising the role he held after David Brown's 2023 exit.

items.title: The July 1 Skyline is out: heat wave edition heads into the holiday weekend
items.summary: Thomas O'Gorman's latest Skyline arrived ahead of Independence Day, with the editor riding out the tropical heat wave 'in front of the air conditioner, set on 67 degrees.' The weekly covers the Loop, River North, Streeterville, Gold Coast, and Old Town; the full paper is available from Inside Publications.

items.title: Raoul praises Supreme Court ruling upholding birthright citizenship
items.summary: Illinois AG Kwame Raoul, part of the multistate suit against the executive order denying citizenship to many born to non-citizens, called the ruling legally significant and personally resonant - he was born in Chicago to a Haitian mother not yet naturalized. Rep. Delia Ramirez praised the decision; Gov. Pritzker and Sen. Durbin warned of continued challenges to immigration policy.

items.title: Supreme Court takes up challenge to Cook County's assault weapons ordinance
items.summary: The U.S. Supreme Court agreed to hear a challenge to Cook County's 1996 ordinance banning the sale, possession, and transfer of assault weapons and large-capacity magazines. State's Attorney Eileen O'Neill Burke said the county 'will not back down' from defending the law. Briefing is expected during the 2026 term.

items.title: Chicago and Cook County minimum wage increases take effect July 1
items.summary: The annual Chicago and Cook County minimum wage hikes took effect today, July 1. Also in the roundup: Pritzker issued a disaster proclamation for 11 storm-hit counties including Cook, and a bill on the governor's desk would raise state payments to the wrongfully convicted to $50,000 per year incarcerated.
